searchDocs.content: Guides

searchDocs.title: On a computer with internet access​
searchDocs.content: helm plugin install https://github.com/databus23/helm-diff

searchDocs.title: On a computer without internet access​
searchDocs.content: Assuming that you're working on your Windows workstation in the Outnet you can install the plugin by copying a few files into your HELM_PLUGINS directory.  Go to the helm-diff gitlab repo.Clone the helm-diff gitlab repo to your workstation.In the root directory you'll find a directory called tar-gz, cd into this directory.Extract the helm-diff-windows-amd64.tgzFind out what's the HELM_PLUGINS path configured on your computer by running helm env, in the out output you'll find all the helm env vars, copy the path.If the HELM_PLUGINS doesn't exist create it.Create a directory helm-diff under the HELM_PLUGINS path.From the extracted tgz file, copy the bin directory and the plugin.yaml into the HELM_PLUGINS/helm-diff directory.  Now you have helm-diff plugin installed on your Windows workstation.

searchDocs.title: How to use it​
searchDocs.content: Assuming that your chart name is foo and your values file name is dev-values.yamlrun the following from the Chart directory you wish to upgrade. helm diff upgrade foo --values dev-values.yaml . You can also specify multiple values files: helm diff upgrade foo --values dev-values.yaml --values dev1-values.yaml . If there's a difference, the output will show all the diffs between the k8s objects which will be modified/create/deleted.  Here's an example of the helm diff output

searchDocs.title: Assumptions​
searchDocs.content: This guide assumes that you have an understanding of creating schemas and already created a schema for your service. Check out the Zero to Hero guide if you haven't created a schema yet.  The examples in this guide will use the schema my-schema with version v1 that was created in the Zero to Hero guide.

searchDocs.title: create your default config​
searchDocs.content: Create a file named v1.configs.json in the same folder as your schema file.  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.configs.json []   Choose a name for your default config:  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.configs.json [ { "name": "default-config", "value": {} } ]   Add the default config value based on the schema:  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.configs.json [ { "name": "default-config", "value": { "id": "default-id", "name": "default-name", "age": 18 "isAlive": true } } ]

searchDocs.title: Run validations​
searchDocs.content: Run the validation script to make sure the default config is valid.  npm run validate

searchDocs.title: Add a reference to another default config​
searchDocs.content: If your default config has a reference to another config you created, or a common config you can reference it in the value:  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.configs.json [ { "name": "default-config", "value": { "commonConfig": { "$ref": { "configName": "other-config", "version": "latest" } } } } ]

searchDocs.title: Next steps​
searchDocs.content: Now that you finished writing your default config, you can submit a PR to the schemas repo and ask for it to be reviewed.  If you have any questions or need help, feel free to ask for help.  If you have any suggestions for this guide, please open a pull request.

searchDocs.title: Bundler pipeline​
searchDocs.content: The bundler pipeline has 1 parameter which is the artifacts.json file.  Here's an example of the artifacts.json  { "docker": { "acrarolibotnonprod.azurecr.io": { "3d-gateway": "v2.4.2", "store-trigger": "v1.5.2" }, "docker.io": { "bitnami/redis": "7.2.3", "timonwong/uwsgi-exporter": "latest" } }, "helm": { "acrarolibotnonprod.azurecr.io": { "gateway": "2.4.2", "store-trigger": "1.5.2" }, "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami": { "redis": "18.5.0" } }, "git": [ "MapColonies/helm-charts" ] }   In order to trigger the bundler pipeline you'll need to follow the steps below:  Go to the bundler pipelineClick on Build with parameters Click on Choose File and upload the artifacts.json from you local computer. Click the Build buttonAfter the bundler will finish it'll send a slack message to the <team>-notifications channel based on the user logged in.Click on the download link in the slack message and download the bundle zip file which you'll use later on in the unbundler in the relevant network.

searchDocs.title: Unbundler pipeline​
searchDocs.content: In order to trigger the unbundler, open the jenkins instance in the relevant network where you want to to unbundle and click on the unbundler pipeline and follow the steps bellow.  Click on Build with ParametersClick on Choose file and select the bundler zip file from your computerClick the build buttonOnce the bundler is done you'll be able to find all the artifacts which were in the bundle zip file in all the registries in the specific network you're working on.

searchDocs.title: Viewing and Managing Active Alerts​
searchDocs.content: While you cannot change the global routing rules, you have full access to the Alertmanager UI to view and manage active alerts.  Open Alertmanager.The main dashboard will show all currently firing alerts, grouped by their labels.If an alert is a known issue being worked on, you can click Silence to temporarily suppress notifications for that specific alert group. This helps reduce alert fatigue for the rest of the team.

searchDocs.title: Requesting Routing Changes​
searchDocs.content: Infrastructure Managed The routing configuration (deciding which alerts go to which Slack channels or emails) is global and centrally managed by the infra team.  Developers currently have read-only access to the existing routing configurations. You do not need to configure routing for your applications yourself.  If your team needs a new Slack channel added, or if you want specific service alerts routed to a different destination, please contact the infra team to discuss adjusting the global configuration.

searchDocs.title: Monitoring Certificates in Secrets​
searchDocs.content: If you have a Kubernetes Secret that contains TLS certificates (for example, a custom Postgres secret), you must explicitly annotate it so the Cert Exporter knows to monitor it.  To do this, add the following annotation to your Secret's metadata:  Annotation Key: cert-manager.io/certificate-name Annotation Value: <your-certificate-name>  Once this annotation is applied, the Cert Exporter will automatically extract the certificates from the secret and begin tracking their expiration dates.

searchDocs.title: Adding a New Endpoint to Monitor​
searchDocs.content: Endpoints are not discovered automatically by the Blackbox Exporter. To instruct the exporter to start probing a new URL, create a Pull Request here with your new or modified endpoint rule and notify Infra team to review and apply the upgrade.  Once merged, Prometheus will automatically reload the configuration and the Blackbox Exporter will begin probing your endpoint.

searchDocs.title: Adding Grafana MCP to an LLM​
searchDocs.content: You can leverage our custom LLM integration to build and modify dashboards using natural language. To do this, you need to configure your LLM client (like Gemini, Claude Desktop, etc.) to use the Grafana MCP server.  Here is an example of the settings you need to add to your agent's configuration file:  { "mcpServers": { "grafana": { "command": "uvx", "args": ["mcp-grafana"], "env": { "GRAFANA_URL": "[https://grafana.mapcolonies.net](https://grafana.mapcolonies.net)", "GRAFANA_API_KEY": "GRAFANA_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_TOKEN" } } } }

searchDocs.title: Monitoring Redis​
searchDocs.content: If you are deploying your own Redis instance, prefer using installed redis-app plugin instead of using redis-exporter  Navigate to the Redis app plugin in the Grafana sidebar.Use the pre-built, ready-to-use dashboards provided by the plugin.Utilize the plugin's custom UI to query your Redis instances directly without needing to write complex PromQL.

searchDocs.title: Method 1: Pushing Logs Directly (Recommended)​
searchDocs.content: The standard and recommended way to get your application logs into our observability stack is by configuring your service to push them directly to our internal Alloy endpoint.  Internal Endpoint: http://infra-alloy  Make sure you configure the appropriate port:  4317 - grpc4318 - http

searchDocs.title: Method 2: Scraping via Pod Labels​
searchDocs.content: This method should only be used for 3rd-party services or legacy applications that do not have the native ability to push logs directly over the network.  To tell Alloy to scrape these pods, add the following custom label to the deployment template:  Label Key: mapcolonies.io/alloy-api-logs Label Value: "true"  Deployment Template kind: Deployment apiVersion: apps/v1 metadata: # Deployment labels - Not here ❌ labels: app.kubernetes.io/name: SOME_NAME spec: template: metadata: # Pod labels - Here ✅ labels: mapcolonies.io/alloy-api-logs: 'true'

searchDocs.title: Querying Logs in Grafana​
searchDocs.content: Since Loki does not have its own standalone UI, all log querying is done through Grafana.  Open Grafana and navigate to the Explore tab (the compass icon in the left sidebar).In the top-left data source dropdown, select Loki.Use the Label filters (e.g., selecting app = my-backend-service or namespace = production) to narrow down your log streams.Click Run query to view the logs.

searchDocs.title: Jumping from Traces to Logs​
searchDocs.content: One of the most powerful features of our observability stack is the seamless integration between Tempo (traces) and Loki (logs).  If you are investigating a slow or failed request using a trace in Tempo, look for a small document icon next to the relevant span. Clicking this icon will automatically open a split pane in Grafana, querying Loki for the exact logs generated by that specific service during the exact timeframe of that span!

searchDocs.title: Ensuring Your Service is Scraped​
searchDocs.content: We utilize standard Kubernetes annotations to instruct Prometheus on what to scrape. To ensure Prometheus picks up your metrics, your pods (not deployments) must reside in one of the monitored namespaces and include these annotations:  prometheus.io/scrapeprometheus.io/portprometheus.io/path

searchDocs.title: Managing Prometheus Alerts​
searchDocs.content: Our Prometheus alert rules are defined centrally as code. These rules use PromQL expressions to evaluate conditions over time.  Alerts Config alerting_rules.yml: groups: - name: blackbox_alerts rules: - alert: CertExpiration expr: ((probe_ssl_earliest_cert_expiry{job="blackbox"} - time()) / 3600 / 24 < 30) for: 90s labels: severity: 'critical' annotations: description: "A certificate ({{ $labels.instance }}) is about to expire in 30 days!" summary: "A certificate ({{ $labels.instance }}) is about to expire in 30 days!"   To add, update, or remove an alert, create a Pull Request herewith your new or modified alert rule and notify Infra team to review and apply the upgrade.

searchDocs.title: Sending Traces​
searchDocs.content: Your services should be configured to send distributed traces exclusively to the External OpenTelemetry Collector.  Service Endpoint: http://infra-otel  Make sure you configure the appropriate port:  4317 - grpc4318 - http

searchDocs.title: Trace Filtering​
searchDocs.content: You do not need to manually filter out health checks or Prometheus scrape endpoints (like /metrics or /health) within your application's telemetry configuration.  Our Internal Collector is configured to automatically drop spans related to these URLs:  healthlivenessmetrics  This ensures our tracing backend remains uncluttered and cost-effective, without requiring duplicate configuration across every microservice.

searchDocs.title: Visualizing Traces in Grafana​
searchDocs.content: Since Tempo acts purely as a backend, all of your trace analysis will happen inside Grafana.  To find and analyze traces:  Open Grafana and navigate to the Explore tab (the compass icon in the left sidebar).In the data source dropdown at the top left, select Tempo.You can query traces in two main ways: Trace ID: If you have a specific Trace ID (e.g., from an error log), paste it directly into the search bar to pull up the exact request waterfall.Search Explorer: Use the visual query builder to search for traces based on tags, service names, or duration.

searchDocs.title: Trace to Logs (Exploration)​
searchDocs.content: When analyzing a trace waterfall, look closely at individual spans. If a small document icon appears next to a span, clicking it will seamlessly pivot you directly to the relevant logs in Loki for that exact moment in time! This is a powerful way to see exactly what an application was logging during a slow or failed request.

searchDocs.title: Standard Deployment Workflow​
searchDocs.content: To trigger a standard deployment, simply push your configuration changes to the master branch.  For example, if you want to update Loki in the development environment:  Modify the infra/loki/dev.yaml file.Commit and push the change to master.The pipeline will automatically detect the change, map it to the infra-loki release, and deploy only that release to the dev environment.

searchDocs.title: File and Directory Structure​
searchDocs.content: To ensure the pipeline detects your changes properly, you must follow the expected Helmfile directory structure. The pipeline supports nested Helmfiles (e.g., a main infra/helmfile.yaml.gotmpl and a sub-helmfile at infra/exporters/helmfile.yaml.gotmpl).  A standard service should be organized like this:  team-folder/ ├── helmfile.yaml.gotmpl # Defines the releases ├── service-name/ │ ├── base.yaml.gotmpl # Base config (applies everywhere) │ ├── dev.yaml # Dev-specific config │ ├── prod.yaml # Prod-specific config │ └── dev/ # Additional dev-specific overrides │ └── additional-config.yaml

searchDocs.title: Running a Dry Run​
searchDocs.content: If you want to preview what the pipeline would change without actually applying anything to the cluster, you can run the pipeline manually in Jenkins and set the DRY_RUN parameter to true. The pipeline will execute a helmfile diff and output the results, but will skip the final apply stage.

searchDocs.title: Troubleshooting​
searchDocs.content: If your deployment does not behave as expected, check the following common issues:  No changes were detected: Ensure the file you modified is located within a monitored team folder and is explicitly listed in the values section of your release in the helmfile.yaml.gotmpl. Also, verify that the filename matches the target environment's naming patterns.Too many releases deployed: Review your file naming conventions. Ensure you haven't accidentally placed environment-specific configurations into a base file, which would trigger deployments globally.A dynamic stage failed: If a stage fails during the apply process, it will not automatically retry. You can manually enable the DEBUG_LOGGING=true parameter in Jenkins to print detailed trace information and matching logic to help identify the root cause.

searchDocs.title: Assumptions​
searchDocs.content: This guide assumes that you have an already existing service based on the MapColonies boilerplate repo, and basic knowledge of JSON Schema. If needed you can read and learn about JSON Schema in the following link: https://json-schema.org/understanding-json-schema.  It is also recommended to read the schemas package readme.

searchDocs.title: Before we start​
searchDocs.content: In general, the implementation of the MapColonies™ Config Management system consists of the following steps:  Work environment setup.Define a JSON Schema for your service configuration.Validating that the schema and the generated types are correct.Integrating the schema with your service. Below each step is explained in detail.

searchDocs.title: Initialize the work environment​
searchDocs.content: Clone the schemas repo into your own machine:  git clone git@github.com:MapColonies/schemas.git   Change the working directory.  cd schemas   Install the dependencies.  npm install   Create a new branch for your schema.  git branch <my-branch-name>   Open the repository in your editor (vscode for this guide).  code .   Create a file for your schema under the schemas folder. The directory hierarchy represents the ID of the schema. For example, a common schema that handles redis configuration might be under schemas/common/redis/v1.schema.json and its id will be https://mapcolonies.com/common/redis/v1. The file name is based on the order of the schema. If it's the first one, the name should be v1.schema.json, otherwise it should be the next number in order.

searchDocs.title: Creating the schema​
searchDocs.content: Start by filling all the metadata for your JSON Schema. The more metadata you fill, it will be easier to understand and use your schema.  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.schema.json { "$id": "https://mapcolonies.com/my-domain/my-schema/v1", "type": "object", "title": "myDomainMySchemaV1", "description": "My domain's schema" }   Create your schema content. Use the official JSON Schema docs, The tips page and check other schemas in the repo for reference.  schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.schema.json { "$id": "https://mapcolonies.com/my-domain/my-schema/v1", "type": "object", "title": "myDomainMySchemaV1", "description": "My domain's schema", "properties": { "id": { "type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the entity" }, "name": { "type": "string", "description": "The name of the entity" }, "age": { "type": "integer", "description": "The age of the entity", "x-env-value": "ENTITY_AGE" }, "isAlive": { "type": "boolean", "description": "Is the entity alive", "x-env-value": "ENTITY_IS_ALIVE" } } }   tip You can use x-env-value to enable overriding the value of a field using environment variable. For more information check out the relevant docs in config and schemas.  If we want our service schema to extend the base boilerplate schema we could do this like that:  { "$id": "https://mapcolonies.com/my-domain/my-schema/v1", "type": "object", "title": "myDomainMySchemaV1", "description": "My domain's schema", "allOf": [ { "$ref": "https://mapcolonies.com/common/boilerplate/v2" }, { "type": "object", "properties": { "myDomain": { "$ref": "#/definitions/myDomainMySchemaV1" } } } ], "definitions": { "myDomainMySchemaV1": { "type": "object", "properties": { "id": { "type": "string", "description": "The unique identifier for the entity" }, "name": { "type": "string", "description": "The name of the entity" }, "age": { "type": "integer", "description": "The age of the entity", "x-env-value": "ENTITY_AGE" }, "isAlive": { "type": "boolean", "description": "Is the entity alive", "x-env-value": "ENTITY_IS_ALIVE" } } } } }    The result is a combined object with both the boilerplate schema and our own properties.  important When extending the boilerplate, you should check and make sure you extend the newest version. The version in this guide might be older.

searchDocs.title: Run validations​
searchDocs.content: We want to make sure that the schema are valid, therefore we need to check the schema for errors. To do so we can run the following command and make changes based on the feedback.  npm run validate   info To see all the validations check the following doc: https://github.com/MapColonies/schemas?tab=readme-ov-file#validations

searchDocs.title: Validating the types​
searchDocs.content: The types that the schemas package exports are automatically generated from the json schema. We want to make sure that the types are correct before we move forward. This way we can check the types and if anything is wrong go back and fix the schema.  Build the package:  npm run build   Check that the types are as you expected. They can be found under the build directory under the same hierarchy as the schema. For example, those are the types generated for the schema we created above extending the boilerplate schema:  build/schemas/my-domain/my-schema/v1.schema.d.ts import { typeSymbol } from '../../symbol.js'; declare const exported: { readonly [typeSymbol]: { [x: string]: unknown; myDomain?: { [x: string]: unknown; id?: string | undefined; name?: string | undefined; age?: number | undefined; isAlive?: boolean | undefined; } | undefined; openapiConfig: { [x: string]: unknown; filePath: string; basePath: string; rawPath: string; uiPath: string; }; telemetry: { [x: string]: unknown; logger: { [x: string]: unknown; level: "info" | "trace" | "debug" | "warn" | "error" | "fatal"; prettyPrint: boolean; }; shared: { [x: string]: unknown; serviceName?: string | undefined; serviceVersion?: string | undefined; hostname?: string | undefined; }; tracing: { [x: string]: unknown; debug?: boolean | undefined; url?: string | undefined; traceRatio?: number | undefined; isEnabled: boolean; }; }; server: { [x: string]: unknown; port: number; request: { [x: string]: unknown; payload: { [x: string]: unknown; limit: string; }; }; }; }; ...   note The rest of the file is important for the inner working of the schemas package. You should check only the types.

searchDocs.title: Checking integration with your service​
searchDocs.content: Before merging the changes you made, we want to make sure it works with your service.  install the development version of the schemas package into your service. There are multiple ways to achieve that, with some of them described below.  GithubLocal PathNPM PackOther Options When opening a PR in the schemas repo, the latest version of your branch will be built and be available for a week. Push your changes to the remote repository. git push --set-upstream origin <my-branch-name> Open a PR in the schemas repo.If all the validations pass, a comment will appear with instructions and link to the package.Insert the link into your service's package.json file. package.json { "dependencies": { "@map-colonies/schemas": <insert-generated-link> } } Install the package. npm install     Change the config in your service to use the new schema.  src/common/config.ts import { type ConfigInstance, config } from '@map-colonies/config'; import { <your-new-schema>, type [your-new-schema-type] } from '@map-colonies/schemas'; // Choose here the type of the config instance and import this type from the entire application type ConfigType = ConfigInstance<[your-new-schema-type]>; let configInstance: ConfigType | undefined; /** * Initializes the configuration by fetching it from the server. * This should only be called from the instrumentation file. * @returns A Promise that resolves when the configuration is successfully initialized. */ async function initConfig(offlineMode?:boolean): Promise<void> { configInstance = await config({ configName: 'boiler-config', configServerUrl: 'http://localhost:8080', schema: <your-new-schema>, version: 'latest', offlineMode: offlineMode }); }   Check that the service works as expected.  note The value you put in place of <your-new-schema> comes directly from the name generated for your schema based on its $id. Every schema exported by @map-colonies/schemas follows this rule: Schema ID: https://mapcolonies.com/my-domain/my-schema/v1 Becomes the exported names:myDomainMySchemaV1 &myDomainMySchemaV1Type So in your service’s config.ts you should write the following in order to match your schema: import { myDomainMySchemaV1, myDomainMySchemaV1Type } from '@map-colonies/schemas';

searchDocs.title: Next steps​
searchDocs.content: Now that you finished writing your schema, you can submit a PR to the schemas repo and ask for it to be reviewed.  You can also check the default configs guide to learn how to define default configs for your schema.  We hope this guide was helpful and you were able to integrate the MapColonies™ Config Management system into your service.  If you have any questions or need help, feel free to ask for help.  If you have any suggestions for this guide, please open a pull request.

searchDocs.title: Phase 1: What to do TODAY​
searchDocs.content: Currently, we are establishing the foundation. Your immediate action items as a development team are:  Build your Helmfile: Begin writing your helmfile.yaml configurations in the new site-values repository.Maintain existing Umbrellas: You may continue to use your umbrellas in the helm-charts repository during this phase. Do not delete them yet!

searchDocs.title: Phase 2: The Upcoming Automation (Target Workflow)​
searchDocs.content: Once the CI/CD pipelines are fully wired to the site-values repository, code promotion will follow this 5-layer loop:

searchDocs.title: 1. Developers Layer (Auto-Dev Loop)​
searchDocs.content: A developer merges a feature PR into the next branch. A GitHub Action builds the artifacts and auto-updates the site-values repo, triggering Jenkins to deploy to dev.

searchDocs.title: 2. QA Layer (Release Candidate Loop)​
searchDocs.content: A team lead manually merges an open release-please PR to create an RC tag. This triggers a PR in site-values for the QA Lead to approve, which deploys the RC to the qa environment.

searchDocs.title: 3. Product Layer (Integration Deploy)​
searchDocs.content: The product team reviews the QA-approved PR and merges it. Jenkins deploys the code to the integration environment.

searchDocs.title: 4. Production Release Layer (Master Deploy)​
searchDocs.content: After Product approval, the RC is merged into master. release-please creates the final tag, and a "Prod PR" is opened in the site-values repo.

searchDocs.title: 5. Customer Package Layer (Shipping)​
searchDocs.content: The Manager merges the Prod PR. Jenkins deploys to prod, and our new GHA bundler automatically creates the .tar.gz archive with all charts, images, and .bundlerrc.json dependencies.

searchDocs.title: Starting a new project using the TypeScript Server Boilerplate
searchDocs.content: Starting a new project using the TypeScript Server Boilerplate When starting a new project based on the boilerplate, you need to change and rename a few files and directories to match your project's name. The following steps will guide you through the process of creating a new project based on the boilerplate. Create a new repository in GitHub. Make sure to set the boilerplate as the template repository.Clone the new repository to your local machine. git clone https://github.com/MapColonies/my-new-amazing-repo.git or if you prefer using ssh: git clone git@github.com:MapColonies/my-new-amazing-repo.git Set the name of the project in the package.json and package-lock.json files. Change the name field to match your project's name. { "name": "my-new-amazing-repo", ... } Edit the README.md file to match your project's name and description. You can also add any additional information that is relevant to your project. Rename any mention of ts-server-boilerplate in the codebase to match your project's name. You should use VS Code or any other IDE to search for the term ts-server-boilerplate and replace it with your project's name. Rename the src/resourceName and src/anotherResource directories and change the content of the files inside them to match your project's resources. You can also add any additional resources that are relevant to your project. Edit the liveness probe in the src/index.ts file to match your project's needs. The liveness probe is responsible for checking the health of the application and returning a response to the client. The liveness probe is configured to return a 200 OK response if the application is healthy, otherwise the application is unhealthy. Run your project to make sure everything is working as expected. npm run start:dev Change the tests to match your project's resources. You can also add any additional tests that are relevant to your project. Run your tests to make sure everything is working as expected. npm run test From here on, you can start developing your project using the boilerplate as a starting point. The boilerplate includes many features and packages to make the development process easier and faster and ensure common practices and standards. Check the boilerplate documentation for more information on the features and packages included in the boilerplate.

searchDocs.title: Knowledge Base
searchDocs.content: Knowledge Base

searchDocs.title: Motivation​
searchDocs.content: The MapColonies™ Config Management system provides a centralized solution for managing all service configurations. This system ensures a single source of truth, simplifying configuration management. It also validates configurations against a JSON Schema, guaranteeing their correctness. Additionally, schema validation ensures that all configurations are strongly typed, enabling seamless integration with your code.  This system will also helps you handle config changes easier. If any of the config has changed, instead of changing the env and redeploy all the deployment, you can just rollout the pod in order to get the new config.  Now you can see the zero to hero doc and integrate this system in your service!

searchDocs.title: What's Next​
searchDocs.content: Customizable Configuration for Testing: We'll add the ability to customize configurations specifically for testing purposes.Enhanced Observability: We'll add improved observability features to facilitate more detailed analysis.UI Improvements: We'll refine the user interface to enhance interaction and usability.

searchDocs.title: What are default configs?​
searchDocs.content: default configs are configs we define when creating our schema that will be deployed to the config-server when the server is started for the first time. This is useful as it makes it easier to manage configurations in a more organized way and make the deployment a breeze.  Default configs support references to other default configs, so you can reuse configurations in multiple places.

searchDocs.title: How do I define a default config?​
searchDocs.content: To define a default config, you need to create a file in the same folder as the schema, with the same version as the schema. For example if the schema is in schemas/my-schema/v1.schema.json, the default config should be in config/my-schema/v1.configs.json.  Each file supports multiple configs, as long as their name is unique across all the configs in the schemas repo.  The file is enforced with the following schema, with the value enforced by the schema of the config:  { "type": "array", "items": { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" }, "value": {} }, "required": ["name", "value"] }, "minItems": 1 }

searchDocs.title: How It Works​
searchDocs.content: Instead of checking a central database for every user request, OPAla uses a faster "bundling" method:  The system watches the database for changes to connections or keys.When changes happen, it packages the new rules into an OPA bundle and uploads it to S3.OPA then downloads these bundles to make fast, local access decisions.

searchDocs.title: System Services​
searchDocs.content: The backend is powered by three main services:  OPA (Open Policy Agent): The core decision engine that runs alongside your services, downloading the latest bundles and enforcing access rules in real-time.auth-manager: The main API used to manage and update authentication data in the database.auth-cron: A background scheduler that checks for database updates and uploads the new bundles to S3.token-kiosk: A dedicated backend service that handles the generation and retrieval of authentication tokens for B2C clients.

searchDocs.title: User Interfaces​
searchDocs.content: You do not need to interact with the backend services directly. You can manage your authentication needs through two simple tools:  Auth UI: A user-friendly dashboard where you can easily search for, create, and manage your authentication data (like clients and connections).    Kiosk UI: A dedicated visual portal specifically for generating B2C tokens securely (ArcGIS-Pro and ArcMap).

searchDocs.title: Security and Logging​
searchDocs.content: OPAla includes built-in features to keep your services secure and easy to monitor:  Masked Tokens: Token details are hidden in OPA responses to prevent sensitive data leaks.Filtered Logs: To help you spot issues instantly, the system logs only show denied requests. This removes log noise and highlights actual security blocks.

searchDocs.title: Our Jenkins Pipeline Stack​
searchDocs.content: We maintain a suite of Jenkins pipelines designed to automate deployments, manage environments, and move artifacts securely.  site-values-deployment: Our core CD pipeline. It intelligently monitors the helm-charts repository and dynamically deploys only the specific infrastructure and application releases that have changed.duplicate-shared-pvc: A utility pipeline that creates a localized shortcut to our central shared ingestion disk, allowing your namespace read-only access to large datasets without duplicating them.bundler: Packages Docker images, Helm charts, and Git repositories into a single secure ZIP file based on an artifacts.json whitelist, preparing them for transfer to air-gapped or restricted networks.unbundler: The counterpart to the bundler. It runs in the target network, extracting the zipped artifacts and pushing them to the local registries.

searchDocs.title: How does it work​
searchDocs.content: When you pass a reference to another config instance, the configuration manager will parse and save the references so it can be resolved later. When the configuration is resolved, the configuration manager will replace the reference with the actual configuration.  For the server to understand a reference it needs to include both the config name and the version of the config. It's also possible to set the version as latest to always get the latest version of the referenced config.  When the final configuration is resolved, the referenced config will be merged with the current configuration under the same hierarchy.

searchDocs.title: Reference structure​
searchDocs.content: In order to use a reference, you need to pass an object with the following structure:  { "$ref": { "configName": "requested-config", "version": "latest" } }   tip In the config-ui config editor, you can press Ctrl + Space to insert a snippet with the reference structure.

searchDocs.title: Example usage​
searchDocs.content: Let's say we want to create a configuration for a database connection for our app. We know there is already a configuration for the database connection in the db-partial config that is only missing the database name.  db-partial { "ssl": { "enabled": false }, "host": "avi" }   We can create a new configuration that references the db-partial config and only sets the database name.  db-full { "$ref": { "configName": "db-partial", "version": "latest" }, "database": "my-db" }   The resolved configuration will be:  { "ssl": { "enabled": false }, "host": "avi", "database": "my-db" }

searchDocs.title: Shared Disk Among Namespaces
searchDocs.content: Shared Disk Among Namespaces Why Do I Need This? Ever needed to access the large, shared ingestion dataset from your development or staging namespace? We have a central disk with all this data, but a Kubernetes limitation prevents sharing a single Persistent Volume Claim (PVC) across multiple namespaces. This Jenkins pipeline is the solution! It creates a special "shortcut"—a new Persistent Volume (PV) and PVC—right in your namespace that points directly to the central shared disk. It’s a quick and easy way to get read-only access to the data you need without duplicating any files. You can access the pipeline easily here

searchDocs.title: Bundler pipeline​
searchDocs.content: The bundler pipeline has 1 parameter which is the artifacts.json file.  Here's an example of the artifacts.json  { "docker": { "acrarolibotnonprod.azurecr.io": { "3d-gateway": "v2.4.2", "store-trigger": "v1.5.2" }, "docker.io": { "bitnami/redis": "7.2.3", "timonwong/uwsgi-exporter": "latest" } }, "helm": { "acrarolibotnonprod.azurecr.io": { "gateway": "2.4.2", "store-trigger": "1.5.2" }, "https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami": { "redis": "18.5.0" } }, "git": [ "MapColonies/helm-charts", "MapColonies/jenkins-integration@multifiles" ] }   The bundler pipeline will bundle all the artifacts (docker tar balls, helm packages, git repos) into 1 zip file.  info Watch out! The bundle will be removed after 1 week!

searchDocs.title: .bundlerrc Feature​
searchDocs.content: Repositories that include a .bundlerrc file in their root directory will be processed with this file as a whitelist. Only the files and directories explicitly declared in the .bundlerrc file will be saved in the final ZIP file. Repositories without a .bundlerrc file will have their entire contents saved in the ZIP file.  .bundlerrc helm/ README.md scripts/

searchDocs.title: Slack notification​
searchDocs.content: Once the bundler is done it'll send a slack message to the <team>-notifications channel based on the user logged in.

searchDocs.title: Unbundler pipeline​
searchDocs.content: The unbundler pipeline is happening in other networks we have. After copying over the bundle file to the relevant network, there will be an unbundler pipeline on the jenkins.  The unbundler pipeline is performing the following:  For each docker tar ball it'll attempt to push the docker image with the same tag to all the docker registries available in all the sites.For each helm package it'll attempt to push the helm package with the same version and name to all the helm registries available in all the sites.

searchDocs.title: System Overview​
searchDocs.content: Jobnik is a distributed job management ecosystem designed to handle complex, hierarchical workflows. It separates the orchestration logic (state management, queuing, persistence) from the execution logic (task processing), allowing for scalable and resilient distributed systems.  The architecture consists of three main pillars:  Jobnik Manager: The central service responsible for state and persistence.Jobnik SDK: The library handling communication, resilience, and type safety.Worker Boilerplate: A standardized scaffolding for building task-processing services.

searchDocs.title: 1. Jobnik Manager​
searchDocs.content: The Jobnik Manager acts as the central orchestration service and the single source of truth for the system. It exposes a RESTful API that enables external services to create jobs, track progress, and manage task queues.  Key Responsibilities:  Hierarchical Data Management: Manages the relationships between Jobs (the root entity), Stages (logical groupings of tasks), and Tasks (atomic units of work).State Management: Enforces valid state transitions using internal state machines. This prevents invalid operations, such as adding tasks to a completed stage or processing a cancelled job.Persistence: Stores all workflow data, status history, and operational metadata in a PostgreSQL database.Queue Management: Implements priority-based task dequeuing logic, ensuring high-priority jobs are processed first.Task Health Management: Monitors and handles 'forgotten' tasks to ensure no work becomes stale. This includes detecting tasks that have been in-progress for too long and recovering from worker failures.Observability: Emits standardized metrics (Prometheus) and distributed traces (OpenTelemetry) to monitor system health and request flows.  Link to Job Manager Repository

searchDocs.title: 2. Jobnik SDK​
searchDocs.content: The Jobnik SDK is a TypeScript library that abstracts the complexity of communicating with the Job Manager. It provides a typed, resilient communication layer used by both producers (services that create work) and consumers (services that perform work).  Key Components:  Producer Client: A client designed for services that initiate workflows. It handles the creation of Jobs, Stages, and Tasks, automatically managing trace context propagation.Worker Client: A client designed for task processing services. It manages the task lifecycle loop: polling for tasks, executing business logic, and updating task status (Completed/Failed) upon conclusion.  Core Capabilities:  Type Safety: Leverages TypeScript Generics to allow developers to define custom schemas for Job, Stage, and Task data. This ensures that payloads passed between Producers and Workers are typed and validated at compile time.Resilience: Built-in implementation of industry-standard stability patterns, including: Circuit Breakers: Prevents cascading failures when the Job Manager is under load.Exponential Backoff: Manages retry logic for polling and network requests.Graceful Shutdown: Handles signal termination to ensure active tasks are completed or released before the service stops. Automated Observability: Automatically injects tracing headers into API calls and exposes worker-specific metrics (e.g., active tasks, task duration, success rates) without requiring manual instrumentation in the business logic.  Link to Jobnik SDK

searchDocs.title: 3. Worker Boilerplate​
searchDocs.content: The Worker Boilerplate is a production-ready template repository designed to accelerate the development of new task-processing services. While not a runtime dependency, it serves as the standard starting point for building services that utilize the Jobnik SDK.  Purpose:  It provides a pre-configured environment that integrates the Jobnik SDK with standard infrastructure requirements, allowing developers to focus solely on implementing the specific business logic for a task (e.g., image processing, data ingestion).  Included Scaffolding:  Dependency Injection: Pre-wired setup using tsyringe for managing service dependencies.Configuration Management: Integration with standard configuration libraries for environment-based settings.SDK Integration: Example implementation of the SDK's Worker client, demonstrating how to register task handlers and define custom types.Deployment Assets: Includes Dockerfiles and Helm charts aligned with infrastructure standards for rapid deployment.  Link to Worker Boilerplate Repository

searchDocs.title: Architecture Overview​
searchDocs.content: Here is a high-level look at how data flows through our system:  Metrics: Application Pods and specialized Exporters expose metrics. Prometheus scrapes these metrics, feeding the data to Grafana for visualization and triggering alerts via Alertmanager.Traces: Applications send traces to the OpenTelemetry Collector (infra-otel), which smartly routes and forwards them to Tempo (backed by S3) for querying in Grafana.Logs: Application logs are picked up by Grafana Alloy (via pod labels or direct push) and sent to Loki (backed by S3) for searching and analysis in Grafana.

searchDocs.title: General Guidelines & Features​
searchDocs.content: Save Dashboards via PR: We are running Grafana v12, which allows you to save your dashboard modifications directly by opening a Pull Request from within the Grafana UI! (We are also working on fully automated dashboard provisioning via sidecar ConfigMaps soon).AI-Powered Dashboards (Grace Period): We've integrated an LLM into Grafana via MCP using a custom service account! You can use it to auto-generate and modify dashboards. Important: During our grace period, the AI is restricted to creating and modifying dashboards only inside the AI folder to keep our core dashboards safe.Alert Routing: Alertmanager handles routing notifications to Slack, classified chats, and email. This global configuration is centrally managed by the infra team. If you need a new route, please reach out. (Heads up: We will be migrating from Alertmanager to Grafana Alerts in the near future!)

searchDocs.title: Service Documentation​
searchDocs.content: Dive deeper into specific components of our stack using the links below:  GrafanaPrometheusTempoLokiOpenTelemetry CollectorGrafana AlloyAlertmanager

searchDocs.title: Exporters​
searchDocs.content: For detailed information on the specific exporters we use to gather metrics, check out the Exporters Directory.

searchDocs.title: Alert Categorization​
searchDocs.content: To keep notifications organized and actionable, our alerts are categorized by:  Severity: Usually classified as critical, warning, or info.Environment: Tagged with production, staging, or development to indicate context.Service Labels: Specific tags like service and team to pinpoint exactly which component is failing.

searchDocs.title: Routing Destinations​
searchDocs.content: Alertmanager is configured to route alerts to several different platforms based on their tags, including:  SlackClassified chat systemsEmail

searchDocs.title: Architecture & Deployment​
searchDocs.content: To keep things simple and highly scalable, we run Alloy as a Standalone Deployment rather than a DaemonSet.  Alloy gathers logs in two distinct ways:  Direct Ingestion (Primary): It exposes an internal endpoint that allows applications to push logs directly to it. This is our standard and recommended approach for all internal services.Dynamic Scraping: It automatically discovers and reads logs from Kubernetes pods using a specific custom label. This method is strictly reserved as a fallback for 3rd-party services that lack the capability to push logs directly.

searchDocs.title: Built-in Filtering​
searchDocs.content: Logs can get noisy, fast. To save on storage costs and keep our search queries fast, Alloy is configured to intercept and drop logs from irrelevant endpoints. This happens at the ingestion layer before the data ever reaches Loki.  The filtered endpoints are:  healthlivenessmetrics

searchDocs.title: Our Exporter Stack​
searchDocs.content: We utilize a carefully selected set of exporters to provide deep visibility into our infrastructure, databases, and managed services without requiring you to write custom metric-gathering logic:  Blackbox Exporter: Actively probes external and internal HTTP/HTTPS endpoints to monitor uptime, response times, and general availability.Postgres Exporter: Gathers deep performance, connection, and health metrics from our central PostgreSQL database.Cert Exporter: Monitors TLS certificate expiration dates across the cluster to prevent unexpected outages.Kube State Metrics (KSM): Listens directly to the Kubernetes API to generate metrics about the state of our deployments, pods, allocations, and nodes.Redis Exporter: Exposes memory, connection, and performance metrics for both central and application-specific Redis instances.

searchDocs.title: Scanning and Discovery​
searchDocs.content: The Cert Exporter is configured by the infrastructure team to scan designated namespaces across the cluster. While it monitors these areas continuously, certain resources—specifically raw Kubernetes Secrets containing TLS certificates—require explicit tagging to be recognized and parsed by the exporter's discovery engine.

searchDocs.title: Primary Modules​
searchDocs.content: While the exporter supports many types of probing, our most heavily utilized configuration is the http_2xx module. This module sends an HTTP GET request to a specified URL and expects a successful 200 OK response. It is our standard way of verifying that web services and APIs are up and serving traffic.

searchDocs.title: Built-in Alerting Capabilities​
searchDocs.content: Beyond simple up/down status, the Blackbox Exporter gathers rich metadata during its probes. For example, when probing HTTPS endpoints, it automatically extracts SSL/TLS certificate details.  We leverage this native capability to power our CertExpiration alerts. By measuring the difference between the current time and the certificate's expiry timestamp provided by the Blackbox Exporter, Prometheus can proactively alert us before a certificate expires, all without requiring any custom exporter logic.

searchDocs.title: Features​
searchDocs.content: Type-safe task handling with full TypeScript support for job and stage definitionsBuilt-in observability with distributed tracing, Prometheus metrics, and structured loggingProduction-ready containerization with multi-stage Docker builds and Helm chartsDependency injection using tsyringe for clean, testable architectureHealth checks and graceful shutdown with @godaddy/terminusComprehensive testing setup with Vitest for unit and integration tests

searchDocs.title: Prerequisites​
searchDocs.content: Node.js >= 24.0.0Connection to a Jobnik Job Manager API instance

searchDocs.title: Installation​
searchDocs.content: npm install

searchDocs.title: Configuration​
searchDocs.content: Configure the worker by editing files in the config/ directory:  default.json - Base configurationdevelopment.json - Development overridesproduction.json - Production overridestest.json - Test environment settingslocal.json - Local overrides (not committed to version control)  Set the Jobnik Job Manager API URL via Helm values or environment variables.

searchDocs.title: Development Mode​
searchDocs.content: npm run start:dev   Development mode enables offline config mode and source map support for better debugging.

searchDocs.title: Production Build​
searchDocs.content: npm run build npm start

searchDocs.title: Running Tests​
searchDocs.content: # Run all tests with coverage npm test # Watch mode for development npm run test:watch

searchDocs.title: Customizing the Boilerplate​
searchDocs.content: This boilerplate includes example "logistics" code to demonstrate task handling. Follow these steps to adapt it to your use case:

searchDocs.title: 1. Remove Example Code​
searchDocs.content: Delete the example logistics implementation:  rm -rf src/logistics src/seeder.ts tests/logistics.spec.ts   Remove the seeder call from src/index.ts:  // REMOVE THESE LINES: const sdk = container.resolve<LogisticsSDK>(SERVICES.JOBNIK_SDK); await seedData(sdk.getProducer());

searchDocs.title: 2. Define Your Job and Stage Types​
searchDocs.content: Create a new types file (e.g., src/yourDomain/types.ts):  import type { IJobnikSDK } from '@map-colonies/jobnik-sdk'; export interface YourJobTypes { jobType1: { data: { /* your job data schema */ }; userMetadata: { /* your job metadata */ }; }; } export interface YourStageTypes { stage1: { data: { /* stage data schema */ }; userMetadata: { /* stage metadata */ }; task: { data: { /* task data schema */ }; userMetadata: { /* task metadata */ } }; }; } export type YourSDK = IJobnikSDK<YourJobTypes, YourStageTypes>;

searchDocs.title: 3. Create Task Handler​
searchDocs.content: Create your manager (e.g., src/yourDomain/manager.ts):  import { injectable } from 'tsyringe'; import type { Task, TaskHandlerContext } from '@map-colonies/jobnik-sdk'; @injectable() export class YourManager { public async handleYourTask( task: Task<YourStageTypes['stage1']['task']>, context: TaskHandlerContext<YourJobTypes, YourStageTypes, 'jobType1', 'stage1'> ): Promise<void> { context.logger.info({ msg: 'Processing task', taskId: task.id }); // Your task processing logic here await context.updateStageUserMetadata({ /* updated metadata */ }); } }

searchDocs.title: 4. Update Worker Configuration​
searchDocs.content: Modify src/worker.ts to use your new types and handler:  import { YourManager } from './yourDomain/manager'; import type { YourSDK } from './yourDomain/types'; export const workerBuilder: FactoryFunction<IWorker> = (container: DependencyContainer) => { const sdk = container.resolve<YourSDK>(SERVICES.JOBNIK_SDK); const manager = container.resolve(YourManager); const worker = sdk.createWorker<'jobType1', 'stage1'>( 'stage1', manager.handleYourTask.bind(manager), config.get('jobnik.worker') ); return worker; };

searchDocs.title: 5. Rename Throughout the Project​
searchDocs.content: Update references to jobnik-worker-boilerplate in:  package.json - name, description, authorhelm/Chart.yaml - name, descriptionhelm/values.yaml - mclabels, configManagement.name, image.repositoryhelm/templates/_helpers.tpl - all template definitionshelm/templates/*.yaml - review all template files for hardcoded references

searchDocs.title: 6. Update Package Metadata​
searchDocs.content: Edit package.json:  { "name": "your-worker-name", "description": "Your worker description", "author": "Your Team" }

searchDocs.title: Metrics​
searchDocs.content: Prometheus metrics are exposed on the /metrics endpoint (default port 8080). Key metrics include:  Worker task processing durationTask success/failure ratesActive task countCustom application metrics

searchDocs.title: Tracing​
searchDocs.content: Distributed tracing can be enabled in config/default.json:  { "telemetry": { "tracing": { "isEnabled": true, "url": "http://your-otlp-collector:4318/v1/trace" } } }

searchDocs.title: Logging​
searchDocs.content: Structured JSON logging is provided by @map-colonies/js-logger. Configure log level:  { "telemetry": { "logger": { "level": "info", "prettyPrint": false } } }

searchDocs.title: Kubernetes/Helm​
searchDocs.content: Deploy using Helm:  helm install your-worker-name ./helm

searchDocs.title: Documentation​
searchDocs.content: Jobnik SDK Documentation@map-colonies/telemetry@map-colonies/js-logger@map-colonies/config  note This page was generated from a remote source. you can find it on https://github.com/MapColonies/jobnik-worker-boilerplate/blob/master/README.md

searchDocs.title: What It Monitors​
searchDocs.content: KSM is configured to track the default, standard Kubernetes resources out of the box. We do not currently use it to expose metrics for Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs).  Because it tracks the cluster state globally, zero developer configuration is required. You do not need to add annotations or configure your deployments to be monitored by KSM.

searchDocs.title: Common Use Cases​
searchDocs.content: KSM is incredibly useful for troubleshooting and optimizing your applications. Here are two primary ways developers leverage KSM data in Grafana:  Tracking Crash Loops: You can build dashboards or set alerts to detect when your Pods enter a CrashLoopBackOff state, helping you catch startup failures or recurring application crashes immediately.Resource Allocation vs. Actual Usage: KSM exposes the CPU and memory limits/requests you have set for your Pods. By comparing these KSM allocation metrics against your actual usage metrics, you can easily spot if your services are over-provisioned (wasting resources) or under-provisioned (risking performance bottlenecks).

searchDocs.title: Monitoring Scope​
searchDocs.content: We maintain a single, centrally managed PostgreSQL instance for the environment. The Postgres Exporter is configured globally by the infrastructure team to connect to this database and expose standard performance metrics (such as active connections, transaction rates, and buffer behavior) to Prometheus.  Because we rely entirely on standard, built-in metrics, we maintain a consistent baseline for database observability without the overhead of maintaining custom queries.

searchDocs.title: Central vs. Developer-Deployed Instances​
searchDocs.content: We have two primary ways Redis is used and monitored in our stack:  Managed Central Redis: The infrastructure team maintains a central, managed Redis instance. The infra redis-exporter is configured exclusively to monitor this specific managed instance.Developer-Deployed Redis: If your team deploys its own custom Redis instances alongside your applications, the exporter is already built directly into the standard deployment chart.

searchDocs.title: Authentication​
searchDocs.content: Access to Grafana is secured and streamlined using GitHub SSO. You can log in using your standard GitHub credentials.

searchDocs.title: Installed Plugins​
searchDocs.content: We have extended Grafana's core capabilities with the following plugins:  grafana-github-datasource: Allows us to pull data directly from GitHub into our dashboards.magnesium-wordcloud-panel: Provides custom visual panels for generating word clouds from log or metric data.redis-app: A comprehensive application plugin that includes ready-to-use dashboards and a dedicated UI for querying Redis directly.

searchDocs.title: AI Integration​
searchDocs.content: We have integrated an LLM directly into Grafana via the Model Context Protocol (MCP) using a custom service account. This allows you to interact with Grafana using natural language to generate or modify dashboards.  To ensure the safety of our core production dashboards, we are currently operating in a grace period. During this time, the AI is strictly restricted to creating and modifying resources only within the designated AI folder.  For instructions on how to set up this connection in your AI client, please see the Adding Grafana MCP to an LLM Guide.

searchDocs.title: Infrastructure as Code (Upcoming)​
searchDocs.content: Currently, alerts and dashboards are managed within the UI. However, we are in the process of migrating to a fully automated GitOps approach using Grafana sidecar ConfigMaps. Once complete, all dashboards and alerts will be provisioned automatically from our infrastructure repositories.

searchDocs.title: Service-Level Scraping Architecture​
searchDocs.content: To improve accuracy and reduce load, our Prometheus is configured to support scraping metrics at the Service level, rather than just the Pod level.  When scraping only at the pod level, metrics representing global cluster states (like a shared cache or global active connections) are duplicated for every replica you have running. By scraping the Service endpoint instead, Prometheus queries the metric just once, providing accurate, deduplicated cluster-wide metrics without artificial inflation.

searchDocs.title: Alerting Engine​
searchDocs.content: Prometheus constantly evaluates defined alert rules against the collected metrics. When conditions are met (such as high CPU usage, certificate expiration, or service downtime), Prometheus fires these alerts and forwards them to Alertmanager for routing and notification.

searchDocs.title: Exporters​
searchDocs.content: Prometheus scrapes data from our own applications as well as a variety of specialized exporters. Documentation for specific integrations (such as the Blackbox Exporter, Postgres Exporter, and Kube State Metrics) can be found in our dedicated Exporters Directory.

searchDocs.title: Two-Tier Tracing Architecture​
searchDocs.content: To efficiently handle scaling and ensure trace integrity, we use a two-tier OpenTelemetry Collector setup:  External Collector (Router): All application services send their traces here. Because this layer scales up dynamically under load, individual spans belonging to the same trace might hit different pods. To fix this, the external collector's primary logic is to route all spans of the same trace to the same internal pod.Internal Collector (Processor): Only the external collector sends data here. Because spans are properly routed, this layer can reassemble the full trace and span hierarchy. It is also responsible for filtering out irrelevant noise—specifically dropping traces for health, liveness, and metrics endpoints—before exporting the clean data to Tempo.  This approach balances high availability with accurate trace assembly and storage efficiency.

searchDocs.title: Metadata​
searchDocs.content: You should use the title and description keywords to provide a description of the schema. This will help users understand what the schema is for.  { "title": "My Schema", "description": "This is a schema for my app" }

searchDocs.title: Examples​
searchDocs.content: You can provide examples of how the schema should be used by using the examples keyword.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } }, "examples": { "name": "John Doe" } }

searchDocs.title: Comments​
searchDocs.content: You can use the $comment keyword to provide comments in the schema. This can be useful for providing additional information about the schema.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } }, "$comment": "This is a schema for my app" }   For more information check the relevant JSON Schema Docs

searchDocs.title: Default​
searchDocs.content: When you have a default value for a property, you can use the default keyword to set it. This is useful for when you want to provide a default value for a property that is not required.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string", "default": "John Doe" } } }   The value will be provided by the config even if it is not set.

searchDocs.title: Required​
searchDocs.content: You can use the required keyword to specify which properties are required in the schema. By default, all properties are optional.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } }, "required": ["name"] }   The types generated for the schema will reflect the required properties.  type MySchema = { name: string; };

searchDocs.title: Enums​
searchDocs.content: You can use the enum keyword to specify a list of possible values for a property. It is not required to specify the type of the property when using enum, so its possible for the enum to be of different types.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "color": { "enum": ["red", "green", "blue"] } } }   The types generated will be a union of the enum values.  type MySchema = { color: "red" | "green" | "blue"; };

searchDocs.title: Refs​
searchDocs.content: You can use the $ref keyword to reference another schema. The references can be either internal or external.  Reference can be used to reuse schemas and keep the schema definitions clean. Even for small schemas like Id, it is a good practice to use refs.

searchDocs.title: Internal Refs​
searchDocs.content: { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "$ref": "#/definitions/name" } }, "definitions": { "name": { "type": "string" } } }

searchDocs.title: External Refs​
searchDocs.content: External refs are only valid to other schemas that are defined in the schemas repository. The way to reference is to use the ID of required the schema.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "$ref": "https://mapcolonies.com/common/schema/v1" } } }

searchDocs.title: Definitions​
searchDocs.content: You can use the definitions keyword to define reusable schemas. The definitions are not part of the validated schema, unless they are referenced.  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "$ref": "#/definitions/name" } }, "definitions": { "name": { "type": "string" } } }

searchDocs.title: Schema Composition​
searchDocs.content: In many cases you will want to compose multiple schemas together. JSON Schema provides a few keywords to help with this. This way you can reuse schemas and keep the schema definitions clean.  The classic example in our case is to extends the boilerplate schema to fit our service.  For more information check the relevant JSON Schema Docs

searchDocs.title: AllOf​
searchDocs.content: The allOf keyword is used to combine multiple schemas together. The properties of the schemas are merged together.  The following schema  { "allOf": [ { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } } }, { "type": "object", "properties": { "age": { "type": "number" } } } ] }   Will be equivalent to the following schema  { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" }, "age": { "type": "number" } } }   important When using additionalProperties: false in the schemas, the allOf keyword will not merge the properties together. Instead, it will require that all properties are present in the schema, Which is not possible. Check the JSON Schema Docs for more information.

searchDocs.title: OneOf​
searchDocs.content: The oneOf keyword is used to specify that only one of the schemas should be valid.  The following schema  { "oneOf": [ { "type": "object", "properties": { "name": { "type": "string" } } }, { "type": "object", "properties": { "age": { "type": "number" } } } ] }   If both properties are provided, the schema will be invalid.  Using oneOf with discriminators​  The discriminator keyword (introduced in JSON Schema draft 2019-09) enhances oneOf by explicitly identifying which property determines the correct schema to use. This is particularly valuable for polymorphic data structures where different object types have different property requirements and default values.  { "storage": { "type": "object", "discriminator": { "propertyName": "provider" }, "required": ["provider"], "oneOf": [ { "properties": { "provider": { "type": "string", "const": "s3" }, "credentials": { "type": "string", "default": "someExampleValue" } } }, { "properties": { "provider": { "type": "string", "const": "fs" }, "credentials": { "type": "number", "default": 100 } } } ] } }   In this example, the discriminator.propertyName value of "provider" indicates which property identifies the appropriate schema. When provider equals "s3", the validator knows to use the first schema (where credentials is a string defaulting to "someExampleValue"). When provider equals "fs", it uses the second schema (where credentials is a number defaulting to 100).  Without a discriminator, the validator would need to try each schema sequentially, which is less efficient and produces less specific error messages. The discriminator pattern also enables type-safe handling of different object variants in strongly-typed languages.  note Note that while the default keyword specifies default values for each variant, not all JSON Schema validators or tools automatically apply these defaults. Some may treat them as annotations only. Check your validation library's documentation for how defaults are handled.  For more documentation on discriminators, see the Ajv JSON Schema documentation.

searchDocs.title: Formats​
searchDocs.content: It is recommended to use the format keyword to specify the format of the string. This will help users understand what the string is for and validate accordingly.  { "type": "string", "format": "email" }   For a full list of formats check the ajv-formats repository.

searchDocs.title: Patterns​
searchDocs.content: You can use the pattern keyword to specify a regular expression that the string should match.  { "type": "string", "pattern": "^[a-zA-Z]+$" }

searchDocs.title: Const​
searchDocs.content: You can use the const keyword to specify a constant value for the string.  { "type": "string", "const": "John Doe" }

searchDocs.title: Architecture & Data Flow​
searchDocs.content: Ingestion: Tempo does not collect traces directly from applications. Instead, it receives all trace data exclusively from our OpenTelemetry Collector.Storage: We use S3 as the backend object storage for Tempo. This allows us to store massive amounts of trace data reliably and cost-effectively.Visualization: Tempo is natively integrated into Grafana. Once the OpenTelemetry Collector sends traces to Tempo, you can view, query, and analyze these traces seamlessly within the Grafana UI.

searchDocs.title: Architecture & Storage​
searchDocs.content: Ingestion: Loki receives log streams primarily from our Grafana Alloy deployment, which scrapes pod logs across the cluster.Storage: We utilize S3 as the backend object storage for Loki. This allows us to store massive volumes of log data securely and cost-effectively without managing complex local storage volumes.Usage Focus: Currently, Loki is used strictly for log search, exploration, and troubleshooting via Grafana. We are not utilizing Loki Ruler for log-based alerting at this time.

The constructorOpt argument is useful for hiding implementation details of error generation from the user. For instance:  function a() { b(); } function b() { c(); } function c() { // Create an error without stack trace to avoid calculating the stack trace twice. const { stackTraceLimit } = Error; Error.stackTraceLimit = 0; const error = new Error(); Error.stackTraceLimit = stackTraceLimit; // Capture the stack trace above function b Error.captureStackTrace(error, b); // Neither function c, nor b is included in the stack trace throw error; } a();   Parameters​  targetObject​  object  constructorOpt?​  Function  Returns​  void  Inherited from​  Error.captureStackTrace

searchDocs.title: API Documentation​
searchDocs.content: This section describes the API provided by the package for interacting with the configuration.

searchDocs.title: ConfigInstance<T>​
searchDocs.content: The ConfigInstance interface represents the your way to interact with the configuration. It provides methods to retrieve configuration values and parts.T is the typescript type associated with the chosen schema. it can be imported from the @map-colonies/schemas package.  Methods​  get<TPath extends string>(path: TPath): _.GetFieldType<T, TPath>​  Description: Retrieves the value at the specified path from the configuration object. Note that the type of returned object is based on the path in the schema.Parameters: path (TPath): The path to the desired value. Returns: The value at the specified path.  getAll(): T​  Description: Retrieves the entire configuration object.Returns: The entire configuration object.  getConfigParts(): { localConfig: object; config: object; envConfig: object }​  Description: Retrieves different parts of the configuration object before being merged and validated. Useful for debugging.Returns: An object containing the localConfig, config, and envConfig parts of the configuration. localConfig: The local configuration object.config: The remote configuration object.envConfig: The environment configuration object.  getResolvedOptions(): BaseOptions​  Description: Retrieves the resolved options from the configuration object. Useful for debugging.Returns: The resolved options, which are an instance of BaseOptions.  initializeMetrics(registry: promClient.Registry): void​  Description: Initializes the metrics for the configuration.Parameters: registry (promClient.Registry): The prometheus registry to use for the metrics.  Configuration Options  This package allows you to configure various options for loading and managing configurations. Below are the available options and their descriptions.

searchDocs.title: Configuration Merging and Validation​
searchDocs.content: The package supports merging configurations from multiple sources (local, remote, and environment variables) and then validates the merged configuration against the schema.

searchDocs.title: Local Configuration​
searchDocs.content: The local configuration is loaded from the path specified by the localConfigPath option. The default path is ./config.

searchDocs.title: Remote Configuration​
searchDocs.content: The remote configuration is fetched from the server specified by the configServerUrl option.If the version is set to 'latest', the latest version of the configuration is fetched. Otherwise, the specified version is fetched.

searchDocs.title: Environment Variables​
searchDocs.content: Configuration options can be overridden by setting the corresponding environment variables as described in schema using the x-env-value key. If an environment variable is set, it takes precedence over the value from the remote or local configuration., If the value of the x-env-format key is json, the environment variable value is parsed as JSON.

searchDocs.title: Merging Configurations​
searchDocs.content: The configurations are merged in the following order of precedence: Environment variablesRemote configurationLocal configuration If a configuration option is specified in multiple sources, the value from the source with higher precedence (as listed above) is used.

searchDocs.title: Validation​
searchDocs.content: After merging, the final configuration is validated against the defined schema using ajv.The validation ensures that all required properties are present, and the types and values of properties conform to the schema.Any default value according to the schema is added to the final object.If the validation fails, an error is thrown, indicating the invalid properties and their issues.  Error handling  This section describes the possible errors that can occur when using the package, along with their codes and payload structures.

searchDocs.title: Identifying errors​
searchDocs.content: The package exposes a helper function called isConfigError to assert what is the error that was thrown and handle it as needed.  import { config, isConfigError } from '@map-colonies/config'; try { const configInstance = await config({ configName: 'boiler-config', configServerUrl: 'http://localhost:8080', schema: commonBoilerplateV4, version: 'latest', offlineMode: false }); } catch (error) { if (isConfigError(error, 'configValidationError')) { console.error('Config validation error:', error.payload); } }

searchDocs.title: optionValidationError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 1Payload: ValidationError[]Description: This error occurs when there is a validation error with one of the configuration options.

searchDocs.title: configValidationError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 2Payload: ValidationError[]Description: This error occurs when the configuration as a whole fails validation.

searchDocs.title: httpResponseError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 3Payload: { headers: Record<string, string>; statusCode: number; body: string; } Description: This error occurs when an HTTP request results in an error response. The payload includes the response headers, status code and body.

searchDocs.title: httpGeneralError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 4Payload: ErrorDescription: This error occurs when there is a general HTTP error. The payload contains the error object.

searchDocs.title: schemaNotFoundError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 5Payload: { schemaPath: string; } Description: This error occurs when the specified schema cannot be found. The payload includes the path of the missing schema.

searchDocs.title: schemasPackageVersionMismatchError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 6Payload: { remotePackageVersion: string; localPackageVersion: string; } Description: This error occurs when there is a version mismatch between the remote and local schema packages. The payload includes the versions of both the remote and local packages.

searchDocs.title: schemaVersionMismatchError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 7Payload: { remoteSchemaVersion: string; localSchemaVersion: string; } Description: This error occurs when there is a version mismatch between the remote and local schemas. The payload includes the versions of both the remote and local schemas.

searchDocs.title: promClientNotInstalledError​
searchDocs.content: Code: 8Payload: ErrorDescription: This error occurs when the prom-client package is not installed. The payload contains the error object.  Debugging  If for some reason you want to debug the package you can either use the getConfigParts or the getResolvedOptions functions described in the API or use the more powerful debug logger.  The package debug logger is implemented using the debug npm package and is configured using the DEBUG Environment variable.  The following are the values you can configure to use the debug option.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=*​
searchDocs.content: Enables all the logs. Note that setting this option might enable debug logging of other packages.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config*​
searchDocs.content: Enables all the logs available in this package.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:config​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to the main logic of the package.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:env​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to parsing environment variables from schemas, and retrieving them for use in the configuration.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:http​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to http requests to the config-server.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:options​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to parsing and validation of the package initialization options.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:schemas​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to the retrieving of schemas.

searchDocs.title: DEBUG=@map-colonies/config:validator​
searchDocs.content: Enables only the logs related to the validation of configurations.  note This page was generated from a remote source. you can find it on https://github.com/MapColonies/config/blob/master/README.md
